title: SEGMENT 17 — Cell-type methylation atlases and deconvolution of cfDNA

lines.text: SEGMENT 17 — Cell-type methylation atlases and deconvolution of cfDNA
Let me come back to the cell-free DNA story from Segment 15 and add one more layer: the use of methylation as a cell-type-of-origin barcode.

This is one of the most practically important applications of the comprehensive methylation atlases that the field has built over the last decade, and it has profound implications for non-invasive diagnostics across many disease areas, not just cancer.
Here is the central observation.

Each cell type in the body has a characteristic methylation pattern, and that pattern includes a set of CpGs that are uniquely methylated or uniquely unmethylated in that cell type and almost nowhere else.
For example, hepatocytes have a small set of CpGs that are unmethylated only in hepatocytes — every other cell type in the body methylates them.
Cardiomyocytes have a different set of cardiomyocyte-specific unmethylated CpGs.
Pancreatic beta cells have their own.
Neurons have theirs.
The cell-type-specific CpGs are like ID tags: a fragment of cell-free DNA carrying an unmethylated hepatocyte-specific CpG must have come from a hepatocyte.
There's no other source.

If you can build a complete catalog of cell-type-specific methylation markers, by doing WGBS on many sorted cell types and computationally identifying the markers, then you can in principle take any cell-free DNA sample — a tube of plasma — and deconvolve it into its cell types of origin.
How much of this DNA came from blood cells?
How much from liver?
How much from heart?
How much from brain?
How much from each of dozens of other tissues?
The composition of cell-free DNA reflects the rate of cell death across tissues, and changes in composition signal tissue damage.
This is the basis for the liquid-biopsy-of-everything vision.
A heart attack causes cardiomyocyte death; cardiomyocyte-derived cell-free DNA spikes in the plasma.
Liver injury causes a spike in hepatocyte cell-free DNA.
Pancreatic beta cell death — the central event in type 1 diabetes — causes a spike in beta-cell cell-free DNA.

Brain injury, organ rejection after transplant, drug toxicity, sepsis — all of these are reflected in shifts in the cell-type composition of cell-free DNA, and methylation deconvolution can read them.

The pioneering work in this space has come from the labs of Yuval Dor and Ruth Shemer at the Hebrew University in Jerusalem, who in a series of papers in the mid-to-late 2010s established the methylation-based cfDNA deconvolution framework and applied it to detecting beta-cell death in diabetes, hepatocyte death in liver disease, and similar applications.
Their work has been complemented by groups at Stanford, Harvard, and several Chinese institutions, building progressively more comprehensive cell-type methylation atlases.
The comprehensive human cell-type methylation atlas project, similar in spirit to the Human Cell Atlas for gene expression, is now well underway.
By 2026, methylation profiles are available for several hundred distinct human cell types, with new ones being added continually.

The atlases enable not just disease detection but tracking of organ-specific responses to interventions — for example, monitoring myocardial recovery after a heart attack, or assessing graft health after a kidney transplant — non-invasively from blood.
The clinical applications are real.

Methylation-based cell-free DNA deconvolution is being used in transplant medicine to detect early organ rejection — donor-derived cell-free DNA in the recipient's blood signals injury to the transplanted organ before it's clinically apparent.
It's being used in prenatal diagnostics to deconvolve fetal versus maternal contributions to cell-free DNA.
It's being used in oncology to detect tumor-derived cell-free DNA even when the tissue of origin is unknown.

The combination of comprehensive methylation atlases, gentle low-input methylation chemistry (EM-seq, TAPS), and sensitive analytical methods (capture-based methylation sequencing for targeted panels, deep learning for cell-type classification) has put us at the threshold of a much broader liquid biopsy era — not just cancer detection but a real-time, non-invasive readout of cell death across the body, with implications for almost every area of medicine.
Methylation is the cell-type barcode that makes this possible, and the methylation methods we've been building up across this lecture are what enable the reading of that barcode at clinical scale.
